name: Thermal Management Framework
summary: A systematic approach to managing heat in rocket systems. From cryogenic propellant tanks at -253 degrees Celsius to combustion chambers at 3,500 degrees Celsius, thermal design is about keeping everything at the right temperature — too hot and it melts, too cold and it becomes brittle.
whenToUseIt: Use this framework when designing any system that operates in extreme temperature environments — engines, re-entry vehicles, cryogenic tanks, spacecraft in sunlight/shadow cycles, or any component that must survive the thermal extremes of spaceflight.
steps: Map all heat sources (combustion, aerodynamic heating, solar radiation, electronics)
Map all heat sinks (radiation to space, propellant boil-off, active cooling)
Define temperature limits for every material and component
Model heat flow paths through the system (conduction, convection, radiation)
Design thermal protection — insulation, coatings, heat shields, active cooling
Analyse transient thermal behaviour (launch heating, orbital thermal cycling, re-entry)
Test in thermal vacuum chamber to validate the model
example: SpaceX's Raptor engine combustion chamber reaches about 3,500 K. The chamber wall must stay below 800 K to maintain structural integrity. Solution: regenerative cooling — supercooled methane flows through channels in the chamber wall, absorbing heat before being injected into the combustion chamber. The methane enters at about 110 K and exits at about 500 K, carrying away enormous amounts of heat while simultaneously pre-heating the propellant for more efficient combustion.
